name: Hoces del Esva I Trail
name_original: Hoces del Esva desde San Pedro de Paredes — circular
description: Hike the difficult 10.1-mile Hoces del Esva I Trail through a stunning gorge with impressive rock walls and lush riverside forests.
region_intro: Hiking around Valdés explores a region characterized by its rugged coastline, green valleys, and river gorges in Asturias, Spain. The landscape features a mix of coastal paths offering sea views and inland trails winding through forests and along riverbanks. Elevation changes vary from gentle coastal strolls to more challenging ascents in the interior. This diverse terrain provides a range of outdoor experiences for hikers. Best hiking trails around Valdés The most popular hiking route is Path of Cabo de Busto — circular, a 5.6 miles (9.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 21 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers scenic coastal views around the cape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Luarca and Northern Route Loop, a moderate 7.1 miles (11.4 km) path. This trail explores the surroundings of Luarca, combining coastal sections with inland paths. Local hikers also love the Hoces del Esva I Trail, a 10.1 miles (16.3 km) trail leading through the Esva river gorge, often completed in about 5 hours 20 minutes. Hiking around Valdés is defined by coastal paths, river gorges, and forested trails. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more difficult, longer routes. The routes in Valdés are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 140 reviews. More than 800 hikers have used komoot to explore Valdés's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Valdés?
A: Valdés offers a wide selection of hiking opportunities, with over 60 routes available. These trails cater to various skill levels, from easy strolls to more challenging treks.

Q: What types of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Valdés?
A: Hiking in Valdés, Asturias, Spain, allows you to explore a diverse landscape featuring rugged coastlines with stunning sea views, lush green valleys, and dramatic river gorges. You'll find a mix of coastal paths and inland trails winding through forests and along riverbanks.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families in Valdés?
A: Yes, Valdés has plenty of easy trails. Approximately 32 of the routes are classified as easy, making them suitable for beginners or families looking for a relaxed outdoor experience. An example of an easy route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14985825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte del Cabo Viewpoint – Punta Arenosa Viewpoint loop from Bustu&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Valdés?
A: Yes, Valdés features several circular routes, which are great for exploring without retracing your steps. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1047542514/path-of-cabo-de-busto-circular" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Path of Cabo de Busto — circular&lt;/a&gt;, offering scenic coastal views.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Valdés?
A: The hiking trails in Valdés are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 140 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, from coastal paths to river gorges, and the well-maintained routes that offer diverse experiences.

Q: Are there any trails that feature waterfalls in Valdés?
A: Yes, Valdés is home to several beautiful waterfalls that can be incorporated into your hikes. You can visit highlights such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/865581" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Nonaya Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2540246" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oneta Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4085144" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Fero Waterfall (Barayo River)&lt;/a&gt;. For more options, check out the guide to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3825587/waterfalls-around-valdes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfalls around Valdés&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What interesting landmarks or natural monuments can I find along the trails?
A: Valdés offers a rich array of landmarks and natural monuments. Along your hikes, you might encounter the picturesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/363182" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Regalina Chapel&lt;/a&gt;, the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3710397" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Luarca Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt;, or the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/93692" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beach of Silence&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4999971" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vidio Lighthouse&lt;/a&gt; also offers spectacular views. For more, explore the guide to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3928990/natural-monuments-around-valdes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Natural Monuments around Valdés&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any challenging or difficult hikes in Valdés?
A: For experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Valdés offers 10 difficult routes. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048597131/hoces-del-esva-i-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hoces del Esva I Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through the Esva river gorge and involves significant elevation changes.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Valdés?
A: While specific seasonal data isn't provided, the diverse landscapes of Valdés, with its coastal paths and inland forests, suggest that hiking is enjoyable for much of the year. Spring and autumn typically offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, while summer is ideal for coastal walks. Always check local weather conditions before heading out.

Q: Are there any long-distance trails or multi-day hiking options in Valdés?
A: While the guide focuses on day hikes, some routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1048392424/northern-route-luarca-and-barayo-reserve-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Northern Route: Luarca and Barayo Reserve Loop&lt;/a&gt; are quite long, extending over 24 km. These longer routes can be challenging and may require more planning, potentially offering opportunities for extended exploration within a single day or as part of a multi-day itinerary if combined with local accommodation.

Q: Can I find trails that offer panoramic viewpoints in Valdés?
A: Absolutely. Many trails in Valdés provide excellent viewpoints, especially those along the rugged coastline or ascending into the interior. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14985825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte del Cabo Viewpoint – Punta Arenosa Viewpoint loop from Bustu&lt;/a&gt; is a great example of a route designed to showcase scenic vistas.
tips: The path leaves the forest and enters Bustiello de Paredes, home to the Hoces del Esva Interpretation Centre. Bustiello is an old cowboy town with traditional hórreos, stone and wood houses, some picturesque buildings and a green and pleasant environment. After the exciting and wild crossing in the Hoces del Esva, the open and serene landscape of the Bustiello pastoral fields seems from another time in any other place. In the center of the village there is a fork in front of a granary with the white and yellow markings of the short-distance path. The route continues to the right, runs along a stretch of road and crosses the Esva river bridge to connect with the concrete track that goes up to Longrey. The houses are coupled on the side of a hill with views of the meadows and the villages scattered in one of the meanders of the Esva. The landscape looks like an artist's canvas due to the perfect distribution of colors, houses, fruit trees, forest patches and small green patches of pasture.

This hydroelectric power station is located on the banks of the Esva river. His works began in 1910, but were not completed until a decade later due to the complexity of the works. It remained in operation until 1972. Currently, part of the original machinery is preserved, which, like its facilities, are abandoned.

The particular geographical characteristics provide the Esva gorge with a particular isolation, favoring the development and conservation of the native vegetation of the environment in an excellent degree of naturalness. Carbayedas (oaks), ashes and alders predominate in the forest of the canyon. The wall of riverside alders is so leafy that it keeps the subterranean tree submerged in a permanent semi-darkness of low light, conducive to creating the ideal habitat for hazelnuts, dogwoods, laurels, ivy, honeysuckle and various types of ferns. Slate, sandstone and quartzite, and to a lesser extent limestone and marble, make up the geological structure of the Hoces del Esva and provide an element of interest to the route from a geological point of view, but they are very slippery rocks when wet. The path runs through several sections of walkways and wooden stairs that go up and down the side of the canyon, sometimes along the riverbank and sometimes at a certain height in the steepest areas. The path passes through the ruins of an old dam and saves a rocky pass with exposure to the void protected by a rope handrail. Further on, the crossroads of trails appears with the variant that descends from El Cabanón.
It is worth visiting the La Vega dolmen, the Ovienes Menhir, the parish church of San Pedro, and the Hoces del Esva.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Hoces del Esva I Trail?
faqs.answer: The Hoces del Esva I Trail is generally classified as difficult. While some sources might rate it as medium, it involves a significant accumulated elevation gain (around 694 meters) and continuous ascents and descents. Some sections require caution due to narrow passages, rope railings, and stairs carved into rock. It's not recommended for very young children, and prudence is advised, especially on rainy days.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail offers a diverse and dynamic terrain. You'll navigate a narrow quartzite gorge with impressive rock walls, sections with wooden walkways suspended over the river, and stairs carved directly into the rock. The path also winds through lush riverside forests and ascends to offer panoramic views. Expect narrow passages, sometimes barely a meter wide, nestled between the river and moss-covered rock faces.

faqs.question: What notable sights or features will I encounter along the route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll experience the spectacular Hoces del Esva gorge itself. You can also discover abandoned rural heritage, such as the old Abandoned Esva River Hydroelectric Power Plant, and lost villages like Bustiello and San Pedro de Paredes. Waterfalls add to the scenic beauty, and informative panels provide insights into the local flora and fauna.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to hike the Hoces del Esva I Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed in various seasons, but it's best to avoid hiking on rainy days due to the challenging terrain and potential for slippery surfaces. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. If hiking on hot days, ensure you bring plenty of water.

faqs.question: Where can I park and start the Hoces del Esva I Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is circular and offers multiple starting points. Popular options include San Pedro de Paredes, Bustiello de Paredes, or Agüera. Parking availability may vary, so it's advisable to check local information or arrive early, especially during peak seasons.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Hoces del Esva I Trail?
faqs.answer: The Hoces del Esva I Trail is located within the Monumento Natural de las Hoces del Esva, a protected area. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for hiking the trail itself. However, it's always good practice to respect local regulations for natural monuments and protected zones.

faqs.question: Is the Hoces del Esva I Trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: The trail's official status regarding dogs is not explicitly stated in the provided information. Given the challenging terrain, narrow passages, and protected wildlife in the Monumento Natural de las Hoces del Esva, it's advisable to keep dogs on a leash and ensure they are capable of handling difficult sections. Always practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: What wildlife can I expect to see on the trail?
faqs.answer: The Esva river basin is known for its exceptional biodiversity. You might spot otters, as it's home to one of Europe's largest concentrations, with an observatory designed for viewing them. Other potential sightings include salmon, trout, eels, deer, and wild boar. The lush riverside forests also host a variety of birdlife.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a hike on this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the trail's difficulty and varied terrain, suitable, preferably waterproof, footwear is essential. Bring plenty of water, especially on hot days, and snacks. A first-aid kit, navigation tools, and appropriate clothing layers are also recommended. Trekking poles can be helpful for stability on ascents and descents.

faqs.question: Is the Hoces del Esva I Trail a loop?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Hoces del Esva I Trail is a circular route, offering the convenience of returning to your starting point without retracing your steps. This allows you to experience the diverse scenery and features of the gorge and surrounding areas comprehensively.

faqs.question: Does the trail connect with other hiking routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Hoces del Esva I (58%), Hoces del Esva II (20%), and various segments of the Ruta Hoces del Esva (totaling 25%). This means you might encounter markers or paths that are part of these other local trails.

waypoints.name: Abandoned Esva River Hydroelectric Power Plant
